data.title: Albemarle Corporation: For Those With High Risk Tolerance
data.description:
ALB stock: previous thesis and new developments

I published the last article on Albemarle Corporation (NYSE:ALB) back in May 2024. The article focused on the technical trading indicators and rated the stock as a sell. To quote:

Market consensus expects Albemarle Corporation to face significant EPS declines in fiscal year 2024. Although I am not as optimistic about the rebound potential after that as the market. I don’t see either lithium overcapacity or EV overcapacity going away in the next 1 or 2 years.

Indeed, the stock price has experienced a significant downturn, losing more than -52%. This contrasts sharply with the SP 500's gain of 13.44% over the same period.

Besides the changes in stock prices (which helped to dissipate some of the risks I saw at that time), there have also been a few other notable developments in its business fundamentals. In the remainder of this article, I will explain why these changes have shifted the return/risk curve of the stock materially and promoted me to adjust its rating to HOLD. Overall, I consider ALB as a high-risk and high-payoff investment under current condition that could be of interest to more aggressive investors.

Let me start with the negative development. Since my last writing, the oversupply of lithium (particularly from the Chinese) as detailed in my last article continued - or even worsened, judging by the pricing environment. To wit, the next chart illustrates the price trend of Lithium Carbonate (CNY/T) over the past five years. As seen, the peaked near 600,000 in later 2022 as the EV industry was experiencing rapid growth. This dramatic climb was followed by an even more dramatic collapse afterwards. The price declined to the ~100,000 level at the time of my last writing and further declined to the current level of 64,700.

Next, I will explain why I think the pricing pressure will persist for longer.

ALB stock: earnings outlook

Let me start with a quick introduction to ALB in case there are readers new to the stock. ALB is in the business of special chemicals. It makes a wide range of chemicals that are additives to or intermediates for plastics, polymers and elastomers, agricultural compounds, and pharmaceuticals. However, in recent years, the company has developed a concentrated exposure to lithium (which contributed more than 67% of its 2024 sales). It is a bet on the secular growth of the EV industry, as Lithium is a key chemical in EV battery production. This is why this article started with an analysis of Li pricing.

Looking ahead, I expect the pricing pressure to persist for the next few years (say 2~3 years) for several reasons. First, as detailed in my last article, I don’t expect the lithium overcapacity accumulated in the past few years to fully dissipate anytime soon. Second, the overcapacity issue is further compounded by uncertain EV demand, which is another headwind that I expect to persist. In particular, I think EV demand will remain softened for the next 2~3 years as the Trump administration shifts emphasis towards fossil fuels from renewable energy sources. Finally, Albemarle’s raw material costs could rise depending on which direction the ongoing tariff disputes eventually settle.

These headwinds are reflected in the EPS projection for ALB as you can see from the following table. Consensus estimates point to having an EPS loss of -$1.37 for the full fiscal year of 2025. The EPS is expected to turn positive in FY 2026. But the recovery is extremely mild with an EPS projection of $1.17 only, translating into an implied P/E ratio of over 52x.

ALB stock: gross margin shows sign of recovering

For investors who have the stomach and also patience, the stock could deliver outsized return potential. As shown in the table above, starting in FY 2027, substantial EPS increases in the triple-digit range are expected. EPS is projected to increase to $4.03 in 2027 representing a remarkable 244% YOY growth, then to $13.00 in 2028, representing a 222% growth YOY. With such growth, the implied forward P/E would fall into the single-digit range (i.e., only 4.76x by FY 2028).

There are indeed several factors that could catalyze such growth down the road. Albemarle is well-prepared when/if Lithium demand recovers. The company has been enhancing its lithium footprint aggressively in recent years. There are very few lithium deposits in politically friendly geographies around the globe. As an example, Albemarle’s efforts to buy Liontown Resources, a Perth, Australia-based lithium miner, have been repeatedly rebuffed. Other western-based lithium producers have also stumbled in their attempts to claim the resource site. As such, I do not believe Lithium production can rapidly catch up when/if the demand picks up. In this case, ALB’s current assets and capacity would stand to benefit handsomely.

Switching to a more near-term catalyst, the gross margin of the company is exhibiting some signs of recovery recently, as you can see from the following chart. To wit, this chart illustrates ALB’s quarterly gross profit margin in the past 5 years from 2016. The gross profit margin has been around its long-term average of 28.71% before 2022 as seen. The margin then almost doubled to a high of ~50% around 2022 with the rise of EV and also Li price as discussed earlier. However, this peak was followed by a sharp decline all the way into the red (with a bottom of almost -30% in 2024). Since then, the margin has gradually pulled back and now hovers around 14.5%.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few additional downside risks are worth mentioning. My analyses above mostly focused on the impacts of EV demand on its Li. However, as mentioned, ALB has other segments as well. These segments can be impacted by fluctuations in commodity prices, overall economic conditions such as tariff rates, and also competition. To further elaborate on a risk mentioned earlier, ALB’s international operations entail geographical and execution risks. These risks could impact its timeline and also budget for ramping up its new lithium production projects, causing potential production delays and cost overruns. Finally, investors also need to monitor its balance sheet, as the company has relied on debt financial heavily to finance its expansion projects in recent years. As shown in the chart below, its total long-term debt has increased from about $2 billion in early 2021 to the current level of over $3.5 billion.

To recap, this reexamination of ALB is triggered by the changes in Li price and EV demand dynamics since my last writing. These developments, together with the stock price correction, have shifted the return/risk profile of ALB materially in my model. Now, I see ALB as a high-risk and high-payoff investment – but one with a neutral risk/return ratio. As such, this article adjusts my rating to hold from my earlier sell rating.

data.title: AppLovin: Ecommerce Traction Points Towards A Long Growth Runway (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
AppLovin's (NASDAQ:APP) Q1 results were extremely strong, helping to offset recent concerns around the impact of tariffs on advertisers. The company's current performance is stronger than the headline numbers suggest though, due to the pedestrian growth and margins of the Apps business, which is now being divested. AppLovin's expansion beyond gaming continues to look promising, which is supportive of a likely advertising growth acceleration in the second quarter. While AppLovin's margins are already high, the company's incremental margins suggest that there is still significant room for improvement.

I previously suggested that most of the gains had already been made. AppLovin continues to surprise to the upside though, and I recently repurchased the stock when it dipped on the back of unfounded scepticism. I also had concerns about the potential impact of Unity's (U) Vector solution, but it now looks like this won't be material any time soon. While returns are likely to continue moderating going forward, I now think the current strength of AppLovin's business will lead to further upside.

Market Conditions

AppLovin's rapid growth suggests that its competitive advantage continues to increase, and I see no reason why this will change in the near-term. Unity recently introduced its Vector advertising solution and has already migrated from the Unity ad network. The company has suggested that customers are already seeing a 15-20% lift in installs and in-app purchases, leading to increased spend. This performance is also reportedly better than expected. The shift is yet to have an impact on Unity's financials though, and current guidance suggests that a growth acceleration won't occur until later in the year at the earliest. Given Unity's lack of scale relative to AppLovin, any real headwinds could be years away, even if Vector is a dramatic improvement.

There should also be a tailwind from Apple (AAPL) being forced to allow alternative payment systems in the App Store, as reduced fees will enable developers to reinvest in user acquisition.

The recent implementation of tariffs has raised concerns about AppLovin's exposure to Chinese ecommerce companies. I tend to think that macro exposure is likely to be fairly modest at this point in time. AppLovin's expansion into web advertising and increased scale could eventually change this though.

AppLovin Business Updates

AppLovin's expansion into ecommerce is probably the most important part of the business at the moment. There is a separate model for this part of the business, which AppLovin is still refining. Early results appear promising though, with AppLovin's ecommerce advertising business having something like 600 advertisers and a 1 billion USD spend run rate. For advertisers spending more than 250,000 USD, churn is also under 3%.

AppLovin currently only provides services to a tiny fraction of the market (mainly mid-market advertisers). The company plans on introducing a self-service dashboard in Q2, which will enable automation and open up AppLovin's platform to a broader group of customers.

AppLovin also recently expressed interest in merging with TikTok Global. The move is based on a belief that TikTok's monetization outside of China is currently quite poor (roughly 20 billion USD revenue from 1.1 billion DAUs), something that AppLovin could potentially change. In fact, AppLovin believes that it could help TikTok to generate something more like 80 billion USD revenue annually outside of China. While this sounds promising, there is execution risk, and the benefit to AppLovin shareholders would ultimately depend on how much it has to pay. Regardless, I tend to view an AppLovin / TikTok merger as a low probability outcome.

AppLovin is also selling its Apps business for 400 million USD cash and a 20% stake in the combined business, with the transaction expected to close in Q2. This is meaningless to the company’s valuation but should help to make the financial strength of its advertising business more apparent.

Financial Analysis

AppLovin generated 1.5 billion USD revenue in Q1, an increase of 40% YoY. Sequential advertising growth was 16%, despite there being fewer days in the quarter, which was attributed to model improvements and the first full quarter of contribution from ecommerce advertisers. Over 90% of AppLovin's advertising revenue still comes from mobile games though. Advertising revenue grew 71% YoY to 1.16 billion USD, while Apps revenue was down modestly.

AppLovin expects 1.195-1.215 billion USD advertising revenue in Q2, a seasonally weaker quarter, representing roughly 70% YoY growth at the midpoint. Given a typical revenue beat, this suggests that advertising growth will accelerate in Q2. Total revenue will depend on heavily on when the sale of the Apps business is finalized.

AppLovin's adjusted EBITDA totaled 1 billion USD in Q1 at a 68% margin. 943 million USD of this came from the advertising business, which had an 81% adjusted EBITDA margin in the first quarter. AppLovin also generated 826 million USD free cash flow in the quarter.

Conclusion

AppLovin's strong growth over the past few years calls into the question the company's future growth prospects. I have had doubts in this regard, simply due to AppLovin's mobile gaming advertising market share. AppLovin believes it still has a lot of room to improve the monetization of inventory though. Recent traction in non-gaming categories also opens up inventory from games monetized primarily through in-app purchases. In addition, success beyond gaming increases the probability that AppLovin can build a meaningful CTV business.

While AppLovin's revenue multiple appears high, this must be viewed in light of the company's strong growth rate and extremely high margins. I estimate that the AppLovin's forward PE ratio is probably only in the 40-50x range, which is actually fairly reasonable given AppLovin's growth prospects. Investors should also take confidence in the fact that AppLovin repurchased and withheld 1.2 billion USD of shares in the quarter, acting aggressively as its share price dropped. While I don't expect AppLovin's revenue multiple to continue expanding, strong growth should continue to see the share price move higher as the year progresses.

data.title: BlackRock Equity Dividend V.I. Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Average annual total returns (%) as of 3/31/25

Data quoted represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown, which assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Insurance fees and charges are not included. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Expenses for Class I shares: Total 0.86%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.67%; Net, Excluding Investment Related Expenses 0.66%. Expenses for Class III shares: Total 1.12%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.92%; Net, Excluding Investment Related Expenses 0.91%.

Refer to (800)-441-7762 for most recent month-end performance.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted a return of 4.45% (Class I shares) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Security selection in the health care, industrials, and consumer discretionary sectors was the largest contributor to relative returns. In contrast, performance was primarily hindered by stock selection in the communication services, materials, and consumer staples sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the financials, health care, and industrials sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the health care and consumer staples sectors, and reduced its exposures to the consumer discretionary and communication services sectors.

Contributors

Stock selection in the health care sector, specifically in the health care providers & services industry, was the largest contributor to relative performance. Security selection in the industrials sector, particularly an underweight position in the electrical equipment industry, contributed positively. Lastly, stock selection in the consumer discretionary sector, specifically in the household durables industry, had a favorable impact.

Detractors

The largest detractor was security selection in the communication services sector, notably in the media industry. Stock selection in the materials sector, particularly in the chemicals industry, negatively impacted relative performance. Lastly, stock selection in the consumer staples sector, namely in the tobacco industry, detracted.

Portfolio management

Tony DeSpirito, David Zhao

Top 10 holdings (%)

Investment approach

Seeks long-term total return and current income.

Further insight

Escalating trade tensions have brought volatility and inflationary pressures to financial markets. Optimism about artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure spending is under scrutiny. Chinese company DeepSeek’s AI development has raised questions about whether massive capital expenditure leads to tangible financial returns, casting doubt on growth stocks’ premium valuations. Weak “soft” economic data adds uncertainty to economic forecasts. These factors have driven a rotation into value stocks, which outperformed growth stocks due to attractive valuations and earnings growth convergence. Despite resilient “hard” economic data, discipline is crucial. High-quality businesses with strong fundamentals and reasonable valuations should provide a buffer against potential policy shocks or earnings disappointments.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock and bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 All data refers to the underlying variable insurance product and not the retail mutual fund of the same name. All returns assume reinvestment of all dividends and capital gains distributions. Total investment returns exclude separate account fees, insurance-related fees and expenses. See the fund's prospectus and the prospectus for the applicable variable insurance product for more information including fees and expenses. 2 The unmanaged, total return S&P 500 Index comprises large-capitalization U.S. equities. It represents about 75% of NYSE capitalization and 30% of NYSE issues.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

©2025 BlackRock, Inc. or its affiliates. All Rights Reserved. BLACKROCK is a trademark of BlackRock, Inc. or its affiliates. All other trademarks are those of their respective owners.

Prepared by BlackRock Investments, LLC, member FINRA.

Not FDIC Insured• May Lose Value• No Bank Guarantee

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Quince Therapeutics: Data In Ataxia-Telangiectasia Scheduled For Early 2026
data.description:
Thesis

Quince Therapeutics (QNCX) is pursuing the treatment of ataxia-telangiectasia, a rare neurodegenerative disorder, which starts in early childhood. I have covered the company in the summer of 2024, and the stock has performed well since then, currently showing about 50% gains since publication.

Since my last coverage, some notable things have occurred. The Phase 3 trial in Ataxia-Telangiectasia (A-T), which had just started enrolling in June 2024, should complete enrollment soon. The company has scheduled topline data for early 2026. There have been several notable insider purchases, four analysts have started covering the stock, the company has received broader patent protection and has presented a webinar on the unmet need in Ataxia-Telangiectasia. Finally, a peer-reviewed paper came out in January 2025 on long-term safety of eDSP, the company’s treatment candidate.

Quince is building its Phase 3 trial on teachings of an earlier failed trial which was successful in the 6-9 year age group. The company’s current pivotal trial enrolls only that patient population for the primary analysis. Quince’s technology platform AIDE, acquired from EryDel, allows a conventional corticosteroid with proven efficacy to be delivered without toxicity.

The stock sold off after the most recent earnings report, which stated that the company planned on enrolling a bit less patients than initially planned, given the low dropout rate, ensuring an 80% power of the study. If additional financing could become available, the company would enroll more patients. Contrary to the initial market reaction, I believe the announcement is actually bullish. All patients have so far enrolled in the open label extension, an 80% power for statistical significance is normal, and several patients will likely enroll in near term.

The company’s last reported cash position is $31.6 million, which should provide runway into early 2026, i.e. coinciding with the expected Phase 3 results. That cash position is close to the company’s current market cap, meaning the market is still not giving this program much credit. I still see a very meaningful upside in case of success. I consider chances for success high as the company’s goal is to do a replicate/ the success that was already seen in the younger-age subgroup of the acquired EryDel’s earlier Phase 3 trial.

Quince Therapeutics

Introduction

Quince Therapeutics is a clinical-stage biotech company with an innovative and leverageable technology treatment platform called AIDE.

AIDE is a treatment platform that allows encapsulation of a drug into a patient’s red blood cells, to alter the drug’s characteristics and ensure continuous delivery and sustained efficacy without toxicity.

For A-T, that treatment candidate was previously called EryDex, and has now been renamed eDSP, the DSP part standing for Dexamethasone Sodium Phosphate or simply dexamethasone. The company is testing eDSP in a Phase 3 trial entitled NEAT in patients with A-T, for which the company has now also opened an open-label extension. By loading dexamethasone sodium phosphate into a patient’s red blood cells, eDSP allows for continuous delivery of efficacious concentrations of the corticosteroid over a month’s time with no toxicity issues.

The next disease that Quince will be targeting is Duchenne’s muscular dystrophy.

The treatment landscape of Ataxia-Telangiectasia

A-T is a rare neurodegenerative disorder linked to mutations in the ATM gene. Symptoms start in early childhood. Ataxia, which is linked to dysfunctions in the cerebellum, is characterized by impaired coordination, balance, and movement. Telangiectasias are dilated capillaries observed in the region of the eyes. The disease comes with neurological as well as pulmonary and immunological dysfunction such as infections.

The disease is often diagnosed before the age of five. By the time of their adolescent age, children are often already wheelchair-bound. Life expectancy of patients diagnosed with A-T is less than 30 years.

Though several trials have taken place, no therapeutic intervention has been approved for the treatment of A-T. Corticosteroids are usually given to patients on an on-and-off basis. They temporarily relieve symptoms, but long-term use carries risks, such as immune suppression, infections, osteoporosis and muscle loss. Amantadine and levodopa/carbidopa have in some cases been efficacious in reducing symptoms of ataxia and loss of motor control. Both amantadine and levodopa/carbidopa are treatments intended for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. An open-label study in Israel had shown 76% of patients to improve significantly with regards to ataxia and involuntary movements. A placebo-controlled trial with levodopa, however, had shown no overall improvement though some patients saw some benefit.

Furthermore, immunoglobulin replacement therapy can be used to enhance immune function, and antibiotics can be used to treat infections, but these are neither disease-modifying, and resistance to antibiotics can develop over time.

For the past decades, use of dexamethasone has remained the therapy that is most often used due to its anti-inflammatory effects, but conventional treatment comes with well-known side effects. Prolonged or high-dose use exceeds toxicity thresholds, leading to adrenal suppression, elevated glucose levels, and immunosuppression. The slide below from Quince shows the fluctuations of dexamethasone levels, surpassing the toxicity threshold, with traditional therapy.

Additionally, chronic use can lead to adrenal insufficiency, which can become life-threatening in the case of abrupt discontinuation. There are several other side effects associated with chronic corticosteroid use, such as hypertension, hyperlipidemia, atherosclerosis, ulcers, and gastrointestinal bleeding.

Use of corticosteroids is therefore often limited to the lowest possible dose for a short duration.

eDSP, formerly EryDex, delivers dexamethasone in a sustained manner without toxicity.

eDSP and Quince’s AIDE platform

AIDE stands for autologous intracellular drug encapsulation technology. The machine incorporating the technology is shown below, mentioning some of its advantages.

eDSP delivers dexamethasone via autologous red blood cells, which act as a storage facility for continuous delivery. These red blood cells are first removed from the patient and, through the AIDE technology, they are subsequently enriched with dexamethasone before being reinfused into the patient.

eDSP is to be administered once a month. Plasma levels of dexamethasone are different when administered in such a way, with longer-lasting efficacy, after an initial peak.

eDSP’s total dose is ~15–20mg, which is released continuously, thereby expanding the efficacy window.

The previous ATTeST trial

The NEAT trial builds on teachings from an earlier Phase 3 trial named ATTeST the Italian company EryDel had done. It is therefore logical to discuss that trial first.

The ATTeST trial evaluated the efficacy of a low dose of about 8.2 mg and a high dose of about 17.4 mg in 176 patients with A-T over a six-month timeframe, with the possibility of a six-month extension, and a later open label extension.

The trial showed that eDSP had a favorable safety profile with few adverse events.

In all patients, including those of the age group 10 years and older, ATTeST showed slowed neurological deterioration on the mICARS rating scale, with a treatment divergence of the high dose to placebo of 1.4 points and the low dose showed similar efficacy. The high dose is the dose currently used in the NEAT trial. A 1.4 treatment difference on mICARS is considered clinically meaningful.

When looking at subgroups, the efficacy was most noteworthy in patients who were 6 to 9 years old. This held true for all possibly relevant rating scales: ICARS, mICARS, or RmICARS. In each instance, the treatment difference was highly statistically significant. On RmICARS, the endpoint for the NEAT trial as per the FDA suggestion, the p-value was 0.009.

The treatment difference to placebo on the mICARS scale in that subgroup of patients which now forms the primary patient population of the NEAT trial was 4.8, which equates to nearly a 19% difference from placebo over 6 months.

An explanation for the pronounced effect of eDSP in that patient population could be linked to faster progression of symptoms during those younger years. mICARS scores start plateauing in patients 10 years and older – which may be related to disease progression but also to the quality of the rating scale. In fact, mICARS is focused on lower limb movement. By the age of twelve, most of the patients cannot walk anymore, and neurological signs of disease progression slow significantly.

In a peer-reviewed paper that came out January 2025 on the longer-term follow up study over at least 24 months of eDSP, data of which were published in the scientific journal Frontiers in Neurology, it was reported 99% of patients reported mild adverse events, that did not cause discontinuation or death. The most notable adverse events were transient pruritis (34%), low serum iron (40%), and anemia (13%).

Quince’s CEO gave this some color in a recent interview:

“So the other thing that was shown really clearly in the ATTeST data is that with this open-label extension, at 3-plus years, we've had no serious safety concerns with this therapy. So unlike systemic corticosteroids that are administered through conventional means, we're just not seeing these typical corticosteroid side effects: diabetes, aggression, weight gain, adrenal insufficiency. Those things are just not occurring in the patients who have been on the eDSP system long term.”

The NEAT trial

The Phase 3 NEAT trial is a randomized, double-blind placebo-controlled trial in patients with A-T, randomizing patients on drug versus placebo in a 1:1 ratio for a period of six months. Patients will receive a total of six infusions, scheduled once every 21 to 30 days.

The trial should enroll 86 patients with A-T who are 6-9 years old. This is the primary analysis population. Additionally, the trial will enroll approximately 20 patients who are 10 years or older. That addition of patients 10 years or older has also been included at the request of the FDA, to potentially allow for a broader approval [webinar, 1h.1min].

The trial started enrollment two months before my initial coverage, in June 2024.

According to the last update of May 2025, 63 participants were enrolled, of which 56 belong to the primary analysis population. All thirty participants have transitioned to the open label extension study, which I believe is a very bullish indicator. Patients would not enroll in an OLE if they did not believe there was benefit. In this case, all of them enrolled in the extension study.

A total of 19 study sites are now activated, and the company expects enrollment to pick up in the coming weeks. Approximately seven participants are scheduled for screening over the coming week, and screening failure rate is as low as 10%. Taking that into account, my total enrollment assumption is 70-80 patients by end of June 2025. Absent additional funding to extend its cash runway beyond the first quarter of 2026, Quince is contemplating an early conclusion of enrollment for its pivotal Phase 3 NEAT clinical trial by the end of June 2025 to align the reporting of topline results with the company’s existing cash runway in early 2026. That enrollment would result in approximately 80% power to determine a statistically significant difference in the primary endpoint. A trial that is 80% powered to detect efficacy in a statistically significant manner is standard.

If additional funding is secured in the near term, Quince intends to continue enrollment as previously planned targeting 86 patients with A-T ages six to nine years old in the primary analysis population. As the company’s expectation is to announce results in early 2026, I believe that is realistic.

The stock sold off after the most recent earnings report, which stated that the company planned on enrolling a bit less patients than initially planned, given the low dropout rate, ensuring an 80% power of the study. If additional financing could become available, the company would enroll more patients. Contrary to the initial market reaction, I believe the announcement is actually bullish. All patients have so far enrolled in the open label extension, and an 80% power for statistical significance is normal. As 56 of the 63 already enrolled patients belong to the primary analysis population, I assume additional enrollment will bring these in the mid 60 patients range. Enrollment of that population is logically the key focus of the company, as it is the pre-specified primary analysis population. The PR also contained a quote from the CEO stating that, assuming positive results from the NEAT trial, the company plans to submit applications for approval in the U.S. and Europe in the second half of 2026. I believe this may be an indicator of the company’s expectation that the trial may succeed.

The company is obviously betting on success in this trial. If there would be an additional financing, I believe it will probably be small.

The primary endpoint of the trial, as per the FDA’s suggestion, is change from baseline on RmICARS, the rescored modified International Cooperative Ataxia Rating Scale [for more detail on this, please see min. 21-28 of the company’s webinar]. The FDA’s thinking here is apparently that the RmICARS more accurately reflects patients’ lower limb function, with more points related to posture and gait.

As I had mentioned in my previous coverage, Quince is making a rerun of a trial that was actually successful in the 6-9 year old patient group. The trial in this patient population showed a meaningful difference to placebo, with statistical significance, and chances are therefore high that the same result can be produced. Quince has meanwhile received the backing from the FDA that the way to approval is open if the trial is successful.

Valuation

In my previous coverage, I mentioned that 4,600 patients are diagnosed with Ataxia-Telangiectasia in the U.S. alone, with the company estimating a $1+ billion global peak commercial potential. I had considered drug pricing of $218,000, similar to what has been seen in other orphan diseases such as Biogen’s (BIIB) pricing of Skyclarys for Friedreich’s Ataxia at $370,000 per year, ACADIA Pharmaceuticals’ (ACAD) pricing of DAYBUE (trofinetide) for Rett Syndrome at $575,000 – 595,000 annually, and Sarepta’s pricing of $3.2 million price tag on a one-time gene therapy for DMD. Meanwhile, there is also Krystal Biotech’s (KRYS) pricing of Vyjuvek’s at $631,000 per year. Krystal Biotech’s valuation of +$4 billion mostly relates to the approval for Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa, with a patient population similar in size as Ataxia-Telangiectasia, and Vyjuvek’s platform potential.

Competitive pressures are fairly low in my eyes. Niagen’s (NAGE) drug Niagen nicotinamide riboside has shown improvement in coordination and eye movements in an open-label Phase 2 trial. ICARS scores in that trial fell from 62.8 to 54.4, but the results were insignificant, with a p-value of 0.23. IntraBio, a private company, is testing IB1001, a drug recently approved for Niemann-Pick disease type C under the brand name Aqneursa, in an open-label Phase 2 study that was completed in December 2024, but showed no efficacy in a double blind, placebo controlled Phase 2 study.

I assumed a market penetration of 60%, probability of success in between 50% and 80%, potential peak turnover of $1.08 billion, with market entry possibly occurring as early as 2027. The launch patterns for the afore-mentioned drugs are also similar, as exhibited below.

Quince has patent protection until at least 2034 globally. A recent notice of allowance covering an innovative method of use for Ataxia-Telangiectasia extended patent protections in the U.S. to 2036.

Quince’s platform potential derives from the fact that the AIDE platform can be used in several diseases where corticosteroid treatment has benefits. There is a large number of such indications, such as pulmonary sarcoidosis, dermatomyositis, Becker muscular dystrophy, juvenile idiopathic arthritis, autoimmune hepatitis, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy, and pediatric lupus.

The primary expansion for eDSP would be Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy. Patients with DMD also face muscle weakness and loss of mobility, and corticosteroids are also usually prescribed to slow muscle degeneration. The long-term side effects of their use, such as adrenal suppression and growth retardation, are similar as in A-T.

Insider purchases

There has been a significant number of insider purchases since my last coverage, as shown below, primarily from the CEO and CMO Dirk Thye and the COO and chief business officer Brendan Hannah. Additionally, Charles Ryan, the president of Quince, has also increased his stake by 65% in August 2024.

There have been no insider sales since my last coverage.

Recent Wall Street analyst coverage

When I first covered the company last year, there was no sell-side analyst coverage. At this time, four analysts are covering the company, namely Oppenheimer, Maxim Group, Brookline Capital Markets, and D. Boral Capital. Their average price target is $7.25, i.e. 659% higher than the current market valuation.

This is remarkable to me because I rarely see such a sudden increase in analyst coverage, with price targets quite far from where the company is currently trading. I assume the reason is Quince showing up on their radar since the initiation of the Phase 3 trial, the strength of the previous ATTeST trial results, and their experience with other approvals in rare diseases.

Financials

As of December 31, 2024, Quince’s cash position was $31.6 million. The company estimates the total costs for the NEAT study and its open label extension to be respectively ~$20 million and ~$15. The company considers that its current cash should be sufficient to provide operating runway through Phase 3 results now expected in early 2026.

Net cash used in operating activities was $31.9 million for the year ended December 31, 2024. From that perspective, I assume that the company’s cash should indeed be sufficient until early 2026.

I do note the existence of an S-3 filing relating to the offer and sale of up to $200 million, and an at-the-market offering for up to $75 million worth of shares, as recently updated. I assume the S-3 could be used to some extent in case of a successful readout.

Risks

Quince's investment thesis relies on one technology, the AIDE technology, and essentially one trial, the NEAT trial of its lead eDSP asset. If the NEAT trial fails, the company may have a hard time finding funding to pursue further promising avenues with eDSP. In that sense, there is no back-up option and risk diversification that would come with this investment. There is always regulatory uncertainty, and the Trump administration has not made that less than before. Additionally, if successful and eDSP would be approved for A-T, the investment thesis relies on high drug pricing, given the small patient population. Finally, Quince’s acquisition of EryDel entailed $485 million in potential total downstream payments, including $60 million in approval milestones, and $395 million in market and sales milestones.

Conclusion

Quince Therapeutics is now a bit more than half a year away from its pivotal readout in Ataxia-Telangiectasia. Its stock is up about 50% since my last coverage, after having seen a valuation that is more than double the current valuation.

Quince’s NEAT trial builds on teachings from an earlier Phase 3 trial which has significant results in the 6-9 age group. My assumption is that Quince will deliver similar results, focusing on this age group in its NEAT trial. In the case of success, I believe that could be a major game-changer for the company.

The market for Ataxia-Telangiectasia is wide open, and Quince’s treatment eDSP builds on teachings of a previous trial. In case of success, I believe the platform potential of the AIDE technology should not be overlooked. Quince is currently eyeing Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy as its next indication.

The company currently has a market valuation close to $40 million, which is very far from multi-billion valuations of other players in the rare disease space. Krystal Biotech’s +$4 billion valuation is a good example, following the recent approval of Vyjuvek. I believe it is for that reason that analysts have recently started to cover the company, with an average price target more than 650% higher than the current valuation.

Quince’s insiders, namely the CEO & CMO, COO & CBO and President of the company, have also recently been purchasing stock, which is an additional bullish indication.

Quince has enough cash to provide runway until the Phase 3 readout, with an at-the-market facility which may provide some additional funding in months to come. A larger S-3 filing also exists, which I believe could be used to a certain degree to provide for additional funding and potentially allow the company’s transitioning to commercialization in the case of a positive readout.

For the reasons above, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on the stock, as I am looking forward to the NEAT trial readout.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD Has A Bright Future Ahead
data.description:
Despite my bullish projections, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) had a very challenging year in 2024, and the start to 2025 was no picnic, either. AMD dropped from its 2024 high of about $230 to just over $75 during the post-liberation day panic selloff. Like many other stocks, AMD has experienced a substantial rebound off the lows, appreciating to roughly $120 recently, representing an increase of approximately 60%.

AMD 15-Month Chart

Unlike many other companies, AMD remains cheap, trading at about 18 times forward (2026) EPS estimates. Perhaps more importantly, AMD has remarkably bright AI prospects ahead.

AMD's recent earnings were considerably better than expected, as AMD beat both top and bottom lines, increased gross margins, and provided better than expected guidance.

Technically, AMD's stock has become overbought. The RSI is now above 70, and the CCI, the full stochastic, and other technical indicators tell a similar story.

AMD could use a pullback in the near term, and a solid buy-in area may be about the $100-105 level gap fill support. Still, despite being mildly overbought and potentially needing a near-term pullback, AMD remains an excellent intermediate and long-term investment, in my view.

AMD's Blockbuster Earnings

I anticipated AMD's Q1 earnings to shine despite the macroeconomic slowdown, and the company didn't disappoint. AMD provided Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $0.96, beating the consensus estimate by 3 cents. Revenue of $7.44B beat the estimates by a significant $320M. Perhaps most impressively, AMD anticipates Q2 revenues to be about $7.4B vs. the estimate of about $7.24B.

Additionally, excluding the $800M export control charge, AMD's non-GAAP gross margin would be 54%, a considerable improvement that illustrates increased profitability potential for AMD, increasing the likelihood of surpassing future estimates.

AI sales surged by 57% YoY to $3.7B, accounting for roughly 50% of AMD's total revenues. Client segment sales increased by nearly 68% to roughly $2.3B, illustrating considerable growth. Generally, AMD's earnings appear very solid, and there is a high probability for more outperformance ahead.

AMD's $10B Partnership With HUMAIN Is A Big Win

In addition to AMD smashing earnings, AMD struck a multi-year $10B deal with Saudi Arabia's AI firm HUMAIN. This dynamic should enable AMD to continue advancing market share in the AI segment, also implying that there may be considerable growth potential ahead for AMD and the general GPU space. Also, BofA noted that while AMD and Nvidia (NVDA) were the greatest beneficiaries of the Saudi deal, Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL) should benefit as well.

Increasing Buyback By $6 Billion

In addition to all the solid news flow, AMD unveiled a new $6B buyback program. This buyback initiative will run in addition to the $4B AMD has left on its previous round. Buying back $10 billion worth of stock is crucial, as it will reduce the number of shares outstanding, leading to increased EPS.

The Bottom Line

AMD now has multiple very bullish factors working for it. We heard the negative drumbeat almost all of last year regarding AMD. There was discussion regarding a potential AI slowdown this year, and almost panic-like conditions regarding AMD. Nonetheless, we see the company performing very well, and its AI segment is doing great.

Additionally, AMD could benefit from its latest AI chips, and we see continued massive demand for general AI products, particularly GPUs. Due to the bullish dynamic surrounding AMD, I have increased my base case one-year target to $175 for AMD. Also, in a more bullish case outcome, we could see AMD's share price climb to about $180-200 within the next year, in my view.

Where AMD's stock could be in the future

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Revenue (in billions) $33 $42 $49 $58 $67 $75
Revenue growth 27% 26% 19% 17% 15% 12%
EPS $4.50 $7 $9 $12 $14.50 $17
EPS growth 36% 55% 33% 32% 21% 17%
Forward P/E 25 26 27 27 26 25
Stock price $175 $235 $325 $392 $443 $490

Source: The Financial Prophet.

When you're buying AMD here, I think it's important to recognize that you are paying for a cheap valuation relative to the company's growth prospects. The Street may still be behind the curve on AMD here, as the investment community has been overly bearish on the stock for a very long time.

Therefore, it could take some time for AMD's stock to reach a fair valuation. AMD could achieve about $7 in EPS next year, which is considerably higher than today's likely lowballed $5.68 consensus estimate figure.

Also, AMD's stock could go through more multiple expansion, as the stock price may deserve a higher P/E multiple, around the 25-30 range instead of the recent 15-20 range, which is abnormally low for a company in AMD's position.

Risks to AMD

AMD must keep its AI growth momentum, as all eyes are on this crucial segment now. Additionally, AMD must continue executing effectively, as it mustn't fall too far behind Nvidia in the AI race. Moreover, AMD must keep a lid on Intel (INTC) in the CPU market, as AMD needs to preserve and expand its market share in laptop, desktop, and server CPUs.

AMD may also want to communicate better with the market, to avoid prolonged downward periods, like the declines in 2024. On the bright side, the stocks appear to have turned the corner, and the April 7th bottom may represent a significant low point for AMD.

data.title: Dollar Tree: Attractive Buy With Signs Of A Rebound
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Dollar Tree, Inc (NYSE: NASDAQ:DLTR) is one of North America's most prominent retail stores, in operation for over three decades. With 15500+ store locations, over 200,000 employees, and more than 193,000 associates, Dollar Tree provides value to customers with its product portfolio, including household supplies, food and snacks, school supplies, and dining materials at low price-points. With the company at a slight discount, Dollar Tree is a pivotal long-term investment with growth potential.

Acknowledging that the company underperformed in the past few years, the Dollar Tree management is keen on rectifying this with the Family Dollar divestiture, with the deal expected to take place in June for $1.007 billion. The deal will ease burden off Dollar Tree’s operating margins, while also enhancing the overall profitability and growth of its flagship stores. The Multi-Price strategy is also a critical growth driver to grow Dollar Tree’s customer base, profitability, and overall margins. With ongoing trade tensions globally, the strategy has successfully offset this headwind and will continue to do so. Therefore, Dollar Tree is a BUY for long-term investors.

Q4 Results & Financial Performance

In March 2025, Dollar Tree reported earnings for Q4 2024, with mixed results. The company reported an adjusted EPS of $2.29, 4.3% higher than the $2.20 analysts projected, a YoY decline of 15.3% from the EPS in Q4 2023. However, this YoY decrease is offset by the fact that FY 2023 had a 53rd week of sales, impacting the overall figures. Meanwhile, Dollar Tree reported net sales of $5.0 billion for the quarter, far lower than the analysts’ estimates of $8.24 billion. While sales may appear significantly lower than analysts' estimates, this is due to Dollar Tree management planning to divest from the Family Dollar brand, with $3.3 billion in sales from discontinued operations from this move. From continued operations alone, however, sales in Q4 marked a YoY increase of 0.7%. According to COO Mike Creedom, the YoY net sales growth reflects “the solid comp performance and strong revenue contribution from noncomp stores.” Dollar Tree also generated an operating income of $629 million in Q4, a 15.2% YoY slide. This reflects the 2.3% reduction in gross margins and 1% rise in the adjusted SG&A rate.

The Dollar Tree management also disclosed guidance for Q1 2025 and FY 2025. The management projects an EPS of $1.10 to $1.25 for the first quarter, and $5.00 to $5.50 EPS from continued operations for FY 2025. This accounts for the $0.30-0.35 reduction in EPS for the fiscal year following the Transition Services Agreement made with Family Dollar. With the Family Dollar divestiture expected to take place in June 2025, Dollar Tree will incur the full year costs and receive reimbursement income from Family Dollar for half the year. Meanwhile, net sales between $4.5 to $4.6 billion are estimated for Q1, while the sales outlook for the year ranges from $18.5 to $19.1 billion. The net sales guidance considers the comparable store net sales growth of 3 to 5%, a healthy growth rate. With lower guidance for 2025 compared to last year, the management explains that “2025 is going to be a transitional year” following the divestiture of Family Dollar. In the wake of the tariff tensions, COO Mike Creedom clarifies that the company has several contingencies to limit the impact on the earnings and overall financials.

Moving on to the margins, the company’s gross margins have improved considerably over the past half decade, rising from 30.5% in 2020 to 35%+ in the last 3 years. Dollar Tree’s gross margins in the last 3 years are the highest since April 2015, the quarter before the Family Dollar acquisition. Meanwhile, other margins across the board have shown volatile trends in the last 5 years. The EBIT and EBIDTA margins spiked up in 2022 from the 2020 baselines of 7.4% and 10.11%, only to decrease by at least 5% and 4%, respectively. On the other hand, Dollar Tree incurred negative net margins in FY 2023 and 2024, largely caused by severe underperformance from Family Dollar chain stores. The net margin is skewed for 2024, reflecting the Family Dollar divestiture at significant short-term losses. However, with the divestiture and increase in 3.0 store locations, the management expects the margins to expand considerably in the coming quarters.

What about Dollar Tree’s efficiency and profitability over the last 5 years? Both the ROA and ROE follow a very similar trend to the company’s net margins, as described earlier: a slight increase from 2020 to 2022, followed by a plummet in 2023 and 2024. For any company, I consider an ROA of at least 5% and an ROE of at least 10% as healthy. In 2020-2022, both metrics were comfortably above their respective benchmarks. In the first half of this period, Dollar Tree maintained decent profitability and optimized its assets. However, slow earnings growth and the costs incurred to sustain the Family Dollar chain significantly affected the company’s overall net income and profitability. Hence, the Dollar Tree management acknowledged this with the divestiture and increase of 3.0 stores. This is designed to improve the company’s resource optimization and profitability in the long run.

Looking at Dollar Tree’s financial position, cash and cash equivalents came in at about $1.26 billion, against $2.43 billion in net long-term debt. This is not too much debt, considering the company has a market cap of $17.94 billion. Dollar Tree has a debt-to-equity ratio of approximately 0.86, slightly above the sector average of 0.77, indicating that Dollar Tree operates on relatively high leverage. Dollar Tree has an interest coverage ratio of approximately 14x, well above the acceptable level of 2x. Hence, Dollar Tree can comfortably pay down interest on outstanding debt. With an EBITDA of $2.04 billion and net debt of $6.57 billion in the last 12 months, Dollar Tree’s net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio was around 3.22. While higher than the 3.0 benchmark, the company’s debt load is not a major concern. Furthermore, Dollar Tree’s credit rating is BBB, which falls in the investment grade category. The debt shouldn’t be a major hindrance for Dollar Tree.

Long-Term Outlook

On March 25, the Dollar Tree management finally decided to divest the Family Dollar business, which will happen in June. Family Dollar will be offloaded to Brigade Capital Management and Macellum Capital Management for $1.007 billion. This is a crucial move that will relieve a significant burden from Dollar Tree’s operations. In 2015, the company acquired Family Dollar for $9 billion, with the objective that the acquisition would grow quickly compared to competitors like Dollar General (NYSE: DG) and Target (NYSE: TGT). Dollar Tree management believed the Family Tree merger would expand its consumer base and reduce costs. However, the merger became a liability on Dollar Tree’s operations, primarily since many Family Tree stores were located too close and ended up offsetting each other’s sales. Contrasting business models and strategies also played a part in reducing the customer base, creating confusion among customers about what to expect from each store, with the brand identity becoming less recognizable. As Family Dollar continued its underperformance, this became a burden on Dollar Tree’s margins with integration costs and capital being invested.

Looking forward, the Family Dollar divestiture will benefit Dollar Tree by reinvigorating a sturdy business model. Dollar Tree can now focus on growing its flagship stores over the long-term. CEO Michael Creedom is confident that the separation will enable Dollar Tree to “achieve its full potential.” Dollar Tree can shift its target customer base from lower-income clients in undeserved urban locations to middle-income customers in suburban locations. According to CEO Creedon, there is higher growth potential and demand from the higher-income group, which will play a key role in enhancing Dollar Tree’s long-term margins and stability. While Dollar Tree will have to endure short-term headwinds from the divestiture, including a $3.3 billion reduction in sales last quarter and lower EPS by $0.30 to $0.35, this will significantly reduce operating expenses and enhance profitability in the long-term horizon. Overall, Dollar Tree’s management expects the operating margin to return to pre-acquisition levels of 12%+, having reported an 11% operating margin in Q3 2024 with the divestiture yet to take place.

While Dollar Tree built a reputation for selling products at the fixed $1.25 price point, the company is implementing the Multi-Price Strategy. This year, the firm will add products at price categories of $3, $5, and $7. This strategy intends to expand its product portfolio without altering prices on existing products and expand the customer base. The chart below demonstrates that the strategy attracted more customers purchasing products in the $3 and $5 price categories. Effectively, this helped Dollar Tree gradually build a higher-income customer base. The company expanded this strategy to nearly 2900 store locations in March 2025 and plans to open 300 new stores this year. Such stores, labelled as 3.0 stores, offer customers value with their wider range of products, enhancing Dollar Tree’s profitability and margins immensely. Additional benefits of the Multi-Price Strategy include maintaining a sturdy customer appeal while mitigating the effects of inflation and cost increases caused by Trump’s tariffs.

While Trump’s tariff policies are a major short-term headwind for Dollar Tree’s revenues, with $15 to $20 million in monthly costs, CEO Creedon revealed that the Multi-Price Strategy has offset the tariff impacts by 90% in Q4. While additional tariffs on selected goods from China, Canada, and Mexico will increase monthly costs by $20 million, Dollar Tree is well-positioned to offset this. Ending FY 2024 with nearly 2900 3.0 stores, Dollar Tree aims to have 5200 stores follow the Multi-Price Strategy by the end of FY 2025. This should enable Dollar Tree to enhance its margins and continue mitigating tariff impacts by 90%+ for the remainder of the year. This year, the Dollar Tree management expects comparable-store sales growth of 3% to 5%, with this metric likely to increase further as Dollar Tree continues converting many locations into 3.0 stores.

Potential Risks

As described above, Trump’s tariffs impacted the company, increasing the prices of several products and overall costs. Approximately 43% of Dollar Tree’s direct imports come from China, a significant component of the company’s overall inventory. A higher cost of goods caused Dollar Tree’s overall costs to rise by $15 to $20 million monthly in the last quarter. Furthermore, as mentioned earlier, tariffs on products from Canada and Mexico will increase monthly costs further, impacting the company’s margins. However, the Dollar Tree management is confident that tariff impacts can be mitigated with the Multi-Price Strategy expansion. On May 12, the US and China agreed to roll back tariffs for 90 days, another mitigating factor for Dollar Tree’s cost of goods. This will lower tariffs on Dollar Tree’s direct imports from China from 145% to 30%, temporarily mitigating this headwind for Q2 and the start of Q3.

With the significant growth of e-commerce businesses in the last 10 years, Dollar Tree faces stiff competition from retail giants like Walmart and Amazon, which threaten Dollar Tree’s in-store presence and customer base. While the company launched its own e-commerce platform in 2009, Dollar Tree encountered obstacles like logistics challenges, competitor websites offering closeout merchandise or discounts, and a complicated shipping process. Competitors like Amazon offer more product variety, convenience with their “add-on” programs, and attract customers from the same target group as Dollar Tree. Additionally, while shipping products with the $1.25 price point, in several cases, Dollar Tree incurred shipping costs greater than the income generated from sales of such items. Overall, the company has been unsuccessful in expanding its presence on e-commerce platforms, potentially holding it back as competitors continue attracting customers who prefer online shopping. As digitalization and e-commerce continue to grow globally, this can potentially offset Dollar Tree’s Multi-Price strategy at in-store locations, no matter how successful the strategy becomes.

Valuation

Moving on to the DCF valuation model, I used a conservative and sustainable long-term growth rate of 3.0%, in the middle of the average growth rate range of 2% to 4%. This is used to project Dollar Tree’s future cash flows. The growth rate factors in the potential headwinds of the tariffs and e-commerce threat. I utilized a 6.0% discount rate to compute the PV of future cash flows up to 2033. As for the perpetual growth rate, I assumed a conservative rate of 2.5%. Considering all this in the DCF model, Dollar Tree’s intrinsic value is approximately $90.48. Based on the DCF estimation, Dollar Tree has an undervaluation of 5.56%.

Let’s now take a look at the Multiples Valuation Model. In the analysis, Dollar General, Target, and BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc (NYSE: BJ) are used. These three competitors operate with business models each tailored to the retail industry, with similar product portfolios and a variety of goods. DG, TGT, and BJ each have P/E ratios of 16.1, 11.29, and 26.41, respectively. Therefore, the average P/E multiple of the competitors was 17.93.

Dollar Tree’s forward EPS is 5.21, which is multiplied by the average P/E of the competitors to determine the company’s intrinsic value. With this valuation model, Dollar Tree’s estimated intrinsic value is $93.43. As of May 13, the company is slightly undervalued by 9.01%, and a P/E multiple of 16.45 below its 5-year average P/E ratio of 18.81. The DCF and Multiples Valuation Models estimate that the company is at a discount of at least 5%, with slight upside from its current share price.

Takeaway

The Dollar Tree management has taken massive strides to overturn its underperformance, with the divestiture deal of Family Dollar and the increase of 3.0 store locations. With the company still undervalued, investors have an excellent chance to invest long-term in this retailer. Dollar Tree may have to contend with rising costs of goods from tariffs and the e-commerce threat from competitors. However, Dollar Tree can mitigate the tariff headwinds and grow its operating margins to its highest levels in a decade. Dollar Tree is well-positioned to grow its flagship stores and profitability. With notable long-term growth potential, I initiate a BUY rating for Dollar Tree.

data.title: VNET: Bearish Setup But Growth Potential
data.description:
VNET Group Inc. (NASDAQ:VNET), one of China's largest internet data center providers, is having a roller coaster 2025. Shares, after languishing near all time lows for months, saw significant interest in early 2025, before crashing back. The bounce back was part of a larger rally worldwide on AI driven demand. The recent sell-off also coincides with overall corrections in tech stocks. Despite the sell-off, VNET remains above its lows of 2024. Let us check whether the fundamentals now deserve investing in VNET, for those investors willing to look beyond the risk headlines.

Earnings Analysis and Trends

Earnings numbers were encouraging for Q4 2024. Revenues grew by 18% and adjusted EBITDA also grew by 64% YoY. Guidance was also positive with revenue and adjusted EBITDA expected to grow by 10%-13% and 15-18% respectively. What was also good to note was a significant increase in capex guidance (by 100-140%) for FY 2025.

The capex increase is a bullish sign of demand increment from AI infrastructure for VNET's core IDC (Internet Data Center) segment, now seeing even higher wholesale interests driven by DeepSeeks breakaway research. In fact Q4 2024 already saw the first signs of breakaway demand from the wholesale sector, justifying an increased CapEx guidance as VNET invests in preparing infrastructure to meet those demands. Backlogs, Pipelines all look healthy.

Balance Sheet Health

The debt profile does not indicate immediate worries for VNET. Earnings are well above interest obligations. Net debt has started to show a reduction in 2024 relative to EBITDA. However, debt leverage still looks a little high. Would have been comfortable if debt to EDITDA ratio was around 4 at least. Particularly as VNET embarks on a CapEx cycle going forward. High debt load could signal refinancing issues in tightening market conditions.

Operating Cash flow is healthy, but future equity strength is strained after issuance of convertible senior notes of $600m in Q1 2024 and $430m more recently. Immediate liquidity issues seem tackled as VNET prepares for growth related investments without the need to raise debt or equity, but future equity dilutions risks, if the company does well, may spook long term investors. Generally, such risk reward tradeoffs are priced into share prices very quickly after such events. I assume future share prices reactions to this development should be less pronounced and triggered in the welcome event of the company doing well and bringing note conversion to equity into play. For now, arguing in defense of the issuance, liquidity concerns are taken off the table.

Scope for Growth

VNET’s network of data centers makes it a key player in China’s digital infrastructure push. In addition to the already ongoing emphasis on enterprise clients and wholesale IDC, future growth opportunities will hinge on serving further AI workloads, stickier contracts with hyperscalers and government bodies. VNET's CapEx cycle in 2025, investing in infrastructure development for its wholesale IDC capabilities will place VNET in a great position to grab such demand as and when traction increases. Rising demand of private deployments of DeepSeek in small to medium enterprises is expected to boost demand for VNET.

Valuation

VNET's valuation is lower than a peer like Equinix (market leader with valuation premium) and GDS (existing relations with hyperscalers). After a valuation expansion commensurate with its price jump, valuations corrected in March 2025. However, the forward EV to EBITDA ratio still looks slightly elevated compared to the last few years average. AI demand green shoots and continued growth and profitability explains a lot of that uptick. Summary, valuation shows no alarms neither opportunity. Contracts with hyperscalers could be a significant rerating trigger for multiple expansion.

Risks and Opportunities

In uncertain times, delisting risks of Chinese equity from US bourses does not look an outside risk anymore. More pertinent and immediate risks arise from China's ongoing crackdown on tech companies though, demanding more scrutiny and restrictions. Not the ideal environment for a tech company like VNET to thrive.

There are optional opportunities too. VNET has had several interests shown from private equity firms in the past. Any go private move along with restructuring or spin-offs could unlock value for existing investors, although that too comes with its own risks of specifics of the transaction.

Speculative Bet on VNET

The short interest as per Seeking Alpha, is ~10%, indicating bearishness. Equally, a support is seen at $5 from where the last bounce back initiated. Any movement below $5 could potentially trigger a short squeeze, particularly given VNET's fundamental positives beyond the risk headlines. With an upcoming earnings result, the short positions could have a say on where the shares move.

I would not recommend a buy VNET to value or long term investors because of several moderate to high concerns. The cash position and equity dilution risks, China tech crackdown and even outside risks of delisting weigh against VNET. I am rating VNET an overall "Hold" because of the bearish setup technically for long term investors till demand starts growing at even faster pace, ideally with interest from hyperscalers.

For the upcoming earnings session, keep an eye on AI fueled growth - demand acceleration will be a key positive. I am expecting a continued revenue growth in the mid teens, anything below that will not be change the hold thesis. IDC services revenues increased by 125% YoY in Q4 2024. A similar growth of ~100% YoY should be seen to gauge the health of the growing expectations from this segment.

Speculative investors can consider a "Buy" because I see growth potential, CapEx spending to prepare for incoming demand and improving topline and margins. Valuations have corrected after recent dilution risks were digested. While not a screaming buy from a valuation perspective, the valuation correction does allow for an entry at these levels. Keep a close watch on rerating triggers like hyperscaler contracts or earnings upticks on the upside and risks mentioned on the downside for exits.

data.title: FTEC: Tech ETFs Are Poised To Hit New High
data.description:
As investors fear begin to turn into a greed amid trade deals and economic certainty, the US stock market is roaring back strongly from the recent low. The uptrend is driven by beaten-down mega and large cap tech stocks due to their production and revenue generation reliance on the world's second largest economy. Furthermore, tech companies earnings growth power and potential rate cuts contribute to their upside potential. The category is also trading at attractive valuations after the recent selloff.

Despite that, choosing the right investment option is critical to generate healthy returns. Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FTEC) is one of the best investment vehicles with a significant concentration in tech giants. I maintain my buy rating on FTEC. Other popular tech-focused ETFs, such as iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW) and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF (IGM), appear attractive due to their exposure to key tech giants from the communication sector.

Stock Market Can Hit New Highs With Tech Stocks Leading the Uptrend

According to the CNN's Fear & Greed Index, greed is currently driving the market compared to extreme fear in the previous two months. In my recent articles during the selloff, I strongly favoured dip buying. Although it's slightly late now, I believe initiating a new position in the high beta ETFs or adding to an existing stake could be a smart strategy. This is because markets are likely to extend the uptrend amid multiple catalysts, including the economic stability, earnings growth power, potential rate cuts and attractive valuations.

The US and China agreed to reverse their recent tit for tat tariffs to de-escalate a trade war. In addition, the world's largest economies seek to conclude a long term trade deal to bring stability in global markets. While a trade deal with China is expected to fuel economic growth, it will also improve investor confidence in tech companies due to their significant exposure to China. This is also reflected in tech-heavy NASDAQ's rebound of 22% from its lowest level in early April.

Furthermore, tech companies' earnings growth power is adding to investor confidence. The tech stocks, particularly mega-caps, enabled the S&P 500 to generate 13.4% earnings growth in the first quarter, topping expectations for a 7% increase.

Of the seven mega caps, six topped March quarter estimates, with most of them were the largest contributors to the S&P 500 earnings growth. These tech giants are Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META) and NVIDIA (NVDA). Other large cap tech companies, such as Broadcom (AVGO) and Netflix (NFLX) were also the largest contributors to the S&P 500 first quarter earnings growth.

The information technology sector delivered 17% earnings growth in the first quarter while communication sector reported 29% year over year increase in earnings.

Tech stocks also appears attractive based on valuations. The recent selloff along with strong earnings growth power cooled their valuations from the recent peak. The technology sector is currently trading around 25x forward earnings compared to the recent peak of 30x. Forward PE of the Magnificent 7 declined to 25.

Moreover, the rate cuts are also expected to contribute to stock market upside and investor confidence in risky assets. In April, inflation plunged to 2.3% and unemployment rate hovered around 4.20%, in-line with the Fed's targeted range. Moreover, a slower economic growth may also persuade the Fed to cut rates. Overall, it appears that the market has bottomed-out with prospects for a strong upside in the second half of 2025.

FTEC is a Solid Option to Capitalize on the Uptrend

Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF is one of the most prominent tech-focused exchange traded funds with $12.5 billion in assets under management and 450K in average shares trading volume. FTEC's portfolio is diversified with nearly 290 stocks from the information technology sector. The fund's top 3 holdings, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and NVIDIA, make up 45% of its entire portfolio weight. FTEC's top 10 represents 60% of its portfolio concentration.

With this portfolio, the fund generated exceptional share price return for shareholders in the past five years. Its returns significantly topped the S&P 500 over the years. As Apple, NVIDIA and Microsoft plays a dominating role in FTEC's overall performance, a trade agreement with China can help its share price to rebound strongly in the coming months. Currently, Apple's shares are down more than 20% from its peak due to a trade conflict.

Apple's 90% of production is based in China while Chinese supplier make up 40% of its total supplies. Similarly, China accounts for more than 10% of NVIDIA's total revenue.

NVIDIA was among the star performers in the past two years due to its blistering revenue and earnings growth. The company was the biggest contributor to the S&P 500 earnings growth in the March quarter. Its earnings per share of $0.89 increased 82% from a year ago period. Besides the top 3 holdings, the fund also held a big stake in Broadcom, Salesforce (CRM), Palantir (PLTR), Oracle (ORCL) and Cisco (CSCO).

FTEC earned a buy rating with a high quant score of 4.22, according to the Seeking quant analysis. SA's quant system relies on the real numbers while eliminating emotions out of investment decisions. FTEC's share price momentum earned an A grade. Technically, ETFs with a high share price momentum are expected to perform well in short to long term. Its risk factor is also likely to improve given declining stock market volatility. FTEC's liquidity factor also received a strong grade.

Where Do the Peers Stand?

Other popular tech-focused ETFs are iShares U.S. Technology ETF and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF. IYW tracks the performance of 145 tech stocks. Like the FTEC, IYW offers a greater exposure to Apple, Microsoft and NVIDIA, making up 45% of its overall portfolio. However, IYW's portfolio also offers a small exposure to tech stocks from the communication sector, such as Meta and Alphabet. Its expense ratio of 0.39% is high while liquidity is stronger than FTEC. It also received a buy rating with a quant score of 4.21.

IGM is one of the best tech-focused ETFs due to its well-balanced portfolio. The fund offers exposure to around 290 tech stocks from the information technology and communication sector. Unlike IYW and FTEC, IGM's portfolio concentration in Apple, Microsoft and NVIDIA is limited.

In addition, it offers a higher concentration in the communication sector. I believe the greater exposure to tech stocks like Meta, Alphabet and Netflix can increase the upside potential. Meta and Alphabet and Netflix were among the biggest contributors to the S&P 500 earnings growth in the latest quarter. IGM outperformed FTEC and IYW year to date and in the last twelve months. Its quant score of 4.32 is also higher than FTEC and IYW.

In Conclusion

As fear and uncertainty is fading amid trade agreements between the largest economies, the markets are poised to recover losses and create a long-lasting uptrend. Therefore, buying the high beta tech-focused investment vehicles, such as Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF, appear to be a great strategy. The tech category's robust earnings growth power and attractive valuations adds to its upside. Other investment options like iShares U.S. Technology ETF and iShares Expanded Tech Sector ETF are also attractive due to their exposure to fast-growing communication sector.

data.title: Siemens Aktiengesellschaft 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Siemens Aktiengesellschaft in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: 2 BDCs Likely To Cut Dividends Soon
data.description:
The current macro environment for publicly traded private credit (i.e., business development companies or BDCs) has been deteriorating since the Fed started to make first base rate cuts.

Since most BDCs provide floating rate loan investments and use leverage to magnify the profit potential (i.e., spreads between cost of capital and the loan investments), lower base rates tend to be net-negative to the earnings. In other words, the benefits of cheaper cost of capital do not exceed or match the negatives of having a lower yielding asset base.

The good thing is that the current base rate levels seem to be sticky and here to stay for quite some time. The Fed has assumed a wait-and-see mode to understand how tariffs materialize in the underlying economic data. And the logic says that they (together with de-globalization efforts) should be inflationary; hence, a case for "higher for longer".

However, apart from the base rate factor, there are multiple other reasons, which have (say, from the start of this year) gradually pushed down the BDC prices across the board. For example, currently, the sector median P/NAV multiple is 0.85x, which means that we can buy many BDCs at a 15% discount to their net asset values. The key reasons or drivers here are:

  • Private credit is becoming more popular among institutional investors that stimulates a massive new capital formation in the sector.
  • The new capital coming in has not been matched with the rising demand. Instead, the demand has weakened due to shallow M&A markets (driven by elevated uncertainty). This has led to spread-tightening in the sector.
  • The risks in the economy have increased, triggering a "risk-off" movement. Namely, BDCs as a high-risk asset class has gone increasingly out of vogue as investors try to de-risk their portfolio holdings amid the elevated uncertainty in the system. The underlying risk or assumption here is that in this kind of environment, BDCs should eventually be recognizing growing non-accruals that should cause significant NAV declines and impaired dividends.

The overall macro backdrop is not positive for BDCs.

And let me insert a quote from Warren Buffett here:

Only when the tide goes out do you discover who's been swimming naked

So, what this kind of environment does is crystallizes investment vehicles that are walking on a thin ice and have not really been properly prepared to sustain value in case of more material headwinds.

For many BDCs the most immediate practical implications will translate to dividend reductions as they will have to synchronize the distributions with declining cash generation levels (e.g., non-accruals reducing cash-flowing loan investment base, spread compression etc.)

In this context, I would like to elaborate on two practical BDC examples that, in my view, are about to cut their dividends already this year.

#1 - Horizon Technology Finance, 17.5%

Horizon Technology Finance (NASDAQ:HRZN) is a venture capital focused BDC that has experienced significant price declined over the past couple of quarters. As dividends have remained unchanged but the price has declined, the yield has jumped higher accordingly. Currently, the offered yield stands at 17.5%, which is one of the highest in the sector. And I would say that it is too good to be true.

Here is why.

The structural backdrop is not favorable for BDCs, just as I mentioned above in the intro section. However, for venture capital BDCs, the current environment brings more severe consequences on the underlying operations. In this case, when we have a risk-off mode in the system and the M&A activity is subdued, it is very difficult to source in healthy deal volumes at the necessary scale and quality. Plus, since VCs usually operate with thinner margin of safety than regular and already established businesses, they are more exposed to corporate default risks.

But, obviously, there are several additional HRZN-specific aspects that have pushed down the share price.

First, in Q1, 2025, HRZN generated a NII per share of $0.27, which is in line with the result achieved in the prior quarter despite the fact that the leverage load has increased. What matters here is that the NII per share does not cover the base dividend of $0.33 per share. It means that HRZN has to use leverage to close the dividend coverage gap. This could work in the short term, but it is clearly unsustainable in the long-run. To solve the issue here, HRZN has to either grow the NII or decrease the dividend. In my view, it will have to be the latter move.

Second, HRZN has a significant difference between the portfolio debt yield and what it can source from the markets. Namely, the new loan onboarding yields come in at just below 13%, while the current portfolio debt yield stands at 15%. Over time, the portfolio yield should converge to the market-level, which implies further headwinds to the NII generation.

Third, HRZN has really no options to utilize additional debt to fund new investments that would produce accretive spreads to the NII generation. Currently, HRZN has a debt to equity of 1.54x, which is the sixth highest (most aggressive) ratio in the sector.

In a nutshell, I do not see how HRZN could solve the dividend coverage shortfall situation without reducing the dividend itself.

#2 - Monroe Capital, 16.3%

Monroe Capital (NASDAQ:MRCC) is another high-yielding BDC that has been experiencing considerable price declines over the past couple of quarters. And even though the share price has plunged by more than 25% on a YTD basis, the dividend has remained unchanged, which has resulted in a quite attractive current yield of 16.3%.

Now, the reason why MRCC has suffered so much is mainly attributable to too aggressive leverage in combination with a weak portfolio quality (i.e., notable non-accruals and unrealized losses).

In my view, the situation here is worse than in HRZN's case because at least HRZN has the embedded potential to monetize some part of its warrant/equity base that could generate tangible non-core cash income.

Let me explain.

MRCC distributes a quarterly base dividend of $0.25 per share. However, the NII per share based on Q1, 2025 result is only $0.19. In the prior quarter, the NII per share was $0.23 (adjusted for some positive one-offs). The trajectory is bad, just as the current situation on the dividend coverage end. The dividend coverage is 0.76x, which is the absolute worst level in the entire BDC space.

The question is whether MRCC can recover from it. In my opinion, it is doable, but only if some serious dividend cuts are made.

Currently, the debt to equity is 1.45x, which gives no room for additional growth. In 2026, MRCC will have to refinance $130 million of fixed rate note that has a yield of 4.7%. It means that MRCC will have to recognize a higher cost of capital in 2026.

The matters are made worse by the presence of a notable payment-in-kind income stream that is effectively a non-cash like item, making the true cash dividend coverage less than 0.76x.

Given these fundamental nuances, I do not really see how MRCC could suddenly start generating higher NII per share to hit a sustainable level of dividend coverage.

The bottom line

Even though I am a BDC-bull, I think that the prevailing macro environment does not bode well for the sector in general.

In practice, it means that the total returns will be lower than usual (say, from 2021 to 2022) across the board. More importantly, this should also trigger many dividend reductions in the sector as the BDCs themselves try to align the lower asset yields with sustainable dividend levels.

HRZN and MRCC are two great examples of where, in my opinion, we will see some dividend cuts very soon.
